summary: Setting SKPaint.Shader to null can free the previous shader's native memory while it is still referenced, causing a fatal crash in concurrent scenarios.

bugSignals.errorMessage: Fatal crash when reusing shader after setting brush.Shader = null

screenshots.description: Fatal crash screenshot showing memory corruption

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SKPaint.Shader setter (binding/SkiaSharp/SKPaint.cs line 204-207) still does not hold a C# reference to the shader object, and the native set operation triggers an unref of the old shader.

analysis.summary: When SKPaint.Shader is set to a new value (including null), the native sk_paint_set_shader call decrements the ref count of the previously assigned shader. In a concurrent scenario, if this races with the Skia native rendering using the old shader, the native memory can be freed while still in use, causing a crash. The C# SKShader binding does implement ISKReferenceCounted but the setter does not coordinate GC lifetime or take any mutex. This is closely related to #1880 (same reporter, same root cause — SKPaint not holding a C# reference to its child objects) but the trigger is explicit null-assignment under concurrency rather than GC collection.

codeInvestigation.finding: The Shader setter calls sk_paint_set_shader with the new handle directly. There is no C# reference stored to the shader, and no thread-safety. When set to null (IntPtr.Zero) while the shader is still needed on another thread, the native unref can free the underlying Skia object.

SKShader implements ISKReferenceCounted — the C# wrapper increments the native ref count on construction and decrements on dispose. However, a second ref from SKPaint's internal sk_sp<SkShader> is decremented when the paint's shader is replaced, so the native lifetime is correct in a single-threaded scenario. The crash arises only under concurrent access where the native unref races with native usage.

keySignals.text: The Shader move operator frees the previous shader, even when the shader is still in use.
keySignals.interpretation: Setting SKPaint.Shader = null causes Skia to release the old shader reference; under concurrent usage this can race with active rendering.

keySignals.text: This is a concurrency issue that only occurs sometimes.
keySignals.interpretation: The crash is non-deterministic and requires concurrent access to the same paint object to trigger.

analysis.rationale: Classified type/bug with high severity because the reporter sees a fatal crash. The Shader setter in SKPaint.cs does not store a C# reference and does not protect against concurrent replacement. This is a real memory-safety issue related to — but distinct from — #1880 (which focuses on GC collecting unref'd shaders). Because the crash is concurrency-dependent and no minimal repro is provided, needs-investigation is the appropriate action.
workarounds: Avoid sharing an SKPaint instance across threads. Create a separate SKPaint per thread.
Hold a strong reference to the shader for the full lifetime of drawing operations before nulling out the paint's Shader property.
Ensure brush.Shader is set to null only after all concurrent drawing calls using that brush have completed.
nextQuestions: Can the reporter provide a minimal reproducible sample that reliably triggers the crash?
Is the SKPaint instance shared across threads, or is only the SKShader instance shared?
Which version of SkiaSharp first introduced this behavior?

resolution.hypothesis: SKPaint.Shader setter should be called only from the thread that owns the paint, or the SKPaint should not be shared across threads. The native Skia ref-counting handles single-threaded lifetimes correctly, but concurrent mutation races with native usage.

proposals.title: Use per-thread SKPaint
proposals.description: Do not share SKPaint instances across threads. Give each thread its own SKPaint configured with the desired shader.

proposals.title: Investigate threading model
proposals.description: Trace whether SKPaint is actually shared across threads in the reporter's scenario, or if the crash is caused by GC collecting the unmanaged shader (#1880 root cause).

resolution.recommendedReason: Not sharing SKPaint across threads is the simplest and most robust workaround and aligns with Skia's documented threading model.

actionability.reason: The crash is real and reproducible intermittently but no minimal repro exists. The related issue #1880 shares the same root cause and is already tracked. Investigation needs to determine if this is a duplicate of #1880 or a distinct concurrency issue.

actions.description: Link to #1880 which reports the same root cause (SKPaint not holding C# references to child objects)
Acknowledge the bug, explain the threading model, provide workaround
